title: Cornish pasty
subtitle: Beef skirt, swede, potato, and onion in shortcrust.
excerpt: Beef skirt, raw swede, raw potato, and onion sealed raw inside a shortcrust D-shaped case and baked long and slow so the filling cooks in its own steam. The original working lunch, filled and crimped one end to the other.
sourceNotes: Protected designation of origin recipe for Cornish pasty (EU PGI 2011). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — Cornish pasty in the pastry chapter. The beef-skirt, swede, potato, onion specification reflects the traditional Cornish recipe documented in local Cornish cookery texts and the PGI specification.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked pasties on a lined tray until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag. Bake from frozen at 180°C fan for 60 to 70 minutes. Baked pasties freeze well too; reheat from frozen at 160°C fan for 20 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry dough can be made the day before and refrigerated. Prepare and refrigerate the filled, unbaked pasties for up to 24 hours before baking.

content.text: The Cornish pasty is one of the most precisely specified regional dishes in Britain, protected by a PGI designation that specifies the ingredients, the shape, the crimping method, and the order in which the filling goes in. Swede, then potato and onion, then raw beef skirt — always raw, never pre-cooked. The filling cooks in its own steam inside the sealed pastry case over a long bake, and the swede (which releases more liquid than the potato) keeps everything moist.

The pastry is shortcrust rather than puff, made with strong bread flour which gives a more robust, workable dough that holds its shape during the long bake and is firm enough to be held in the hand. The crimp runs along the top edge, not the side — side-crimping is associated with Devonshire pasties, not Cornish.

attrs.title: On beef skirt
attrs.body: Beef skirt (also called bavette) is the correct cut for a Cornish pasty — it has enough fat and connective tissue to stay moist and flavoursome through the long bake. If skirt is unavailable, beef chuck cut into small dice is the closest substitute. Rump or sirloin are too lean and will tighten and dry during the cook.

items.prepNote: or beef skirt, cut into 1 cm dice
peeled, very thinly sliced or diced into 5 mm pieces
in small pieces for the filling
beaten with a splash of water, for egg wash

content.text: Put {{strong-bread-flour}} of strong bread flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt in a large bowl. Add {{lard}} of cold lard cubes and {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter cubes. Rub them in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized pieces still visible. Add 120 to 140 ml of cold water, one tablespoon at a time, mixing with a knife until the dough just comes together. It should be slightly stiff, not sticky. Divide into four equal portions, flatten into discs, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Mix {{beef-chuck}} of diced beef, {{swede}} of diced swede, {{potato}} of finely sliced or diced potato, and {{onion}} of finely diced onion in a bowl. Season generously with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. The filling is assembled raw.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Roll each pastry disc to a circle about 25 cm in diameter and 4 mm thick. Use a large plate or bowl as a guide and cut around it. Lay the circles on lined baking trays.

Place a quarter of the filling on one half of each pastry circle, keeping a 2 cm border clear. Dot with small pieces of the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Fold the empty half of the pastry over the filling to form a D shape. Press the edges firmly together. Starting at one end, crimp the edge by folding and pressing the pastry in a rope pattern along the top. The crimp should run along the uppermost edge, not the side. Brush all over with beaten {{eggs}} egg.

Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until the pastry is a rich, even amber. The long, lower-temperature bake ensures the filling cooks through fully. Check the underside of the pasty at 40 minutes: it should be firm and lightly coloured. Rest for 10 minutes before eating — the filling is extremely hot inside.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: The raw-filling method has specific failure modes.

items.symptom: Filling raw and watery in the centre
items.cause: Potato or swede cut too thick, or bake time too short.
items.fix: Cut potato and swede no thicker than 5 mm. The full 55-minute bake at 180°C fan is needed to cook the filling through.

items.symptom: Pastry cracked or split during baking
items.cause: Crimped too thin, or vent not made.
items.fix: Make a small knife cut in the top of each pasty before baking to let steam escape. Press the crimp firmly.

items.cause: Oven too low, or pasty underbaked.
items.fix: The pastry should be a deep amber by 55 minutes. If still pale, give 5 to 10 more minutes.

items.cause: Underseasoned — the raw filling needs bold seasoning because the salt distributes during baking.
items.fix: Season the raw filling generously. The two teaspoons of black pepper and the salt are not excessive — they balance the unseasoned vegetables.

content.text: The Cornish pasty was the working lunch of Cornish tin and copper miners from at least the eighteenth century: a self-contained meal, robust enough to survive a journey to the mine, the thick crimp used as a handle to be discarded when the pasty was finished — a hygiene measure, not a waste. The PGI protection given in 2011 establishes the official ingredients and method. The traditional insistence on raw filling, specific cuts of vegetable, and top-edge crimping distinguishes the genuine article from the hundreds of derivatives sold elsewhere. It is best eaten warm, at slightly above room temperature, in both hands.
